text: You can't do this with VS Code's built-in Outline alone, because Outline is based on language symbols (class/function/method), not arbitrary comments. What you can do yourself: Option 1 Like in the previous answer, define region comments in Python: ` `class Class: # region First methods def a(self): pass def b(self): pass def c(self): pass # endregion # region Other methods def d(self): pass def e(self): pass # endregion ` ` Install an extension like outline-sections that maps those comment regions into Outline entries (since Pylance itself won’t group methods by plain comments in Outline). Note the suggested extension here has a different behavior from those suggested in another answer, i.e., this one modifies built-in outline directly. text: scale Python ETLs without rewriting everything in SQL? You said the quiet part out loud. Relational databases are a mature technology at this point; decades old. The reason people put their rows into an RDBMS is to outsource their memory management concerns. You don't care how many rows are in a given table. If the result set is small, an index will quickly give you the result set. If it is big, well, then you just wait on I/O delays, same as any other approach would make you wait. Don't sweat the details. The query planner is there for a reason. Let the backend DB worry about how much RAM and how many data rows are important to the set of result rows.

text: The core problem is you're loading full tables into memory at once. Fix that, solved. Two changes gets you like 80% of the way there: Extract in chunks, not all at once ` `for chunk in pd.read_sql(query, engine, chunksize=50_000): process(chunk) # never holds more than 50k rows at a time ` ` Swap Pandas for Polars. Polars reads lazy, so it only loads what your transformations actually need. Syntax is similar enough that most of your logic ports with pretty minor edits. ` `df = ( pl.scan_parquet("s3://bucket/data.parquet") .filter(pl.col("status") == "active") .select(["id", "amount"]) .collect() # executes here, reads only what's needed ) ` ` S3 + Parquet is worth doing, columnar format means you only read the columns you need, and file sizes drop 3x to 5x with compression. But remember it's an enhancement on top of the above two fixes, not the start. But at 2 million rows, you don't need a distributed system. You need to stop loading everything at once. Cheers.

text: This is not an issue of PyCharm: `typing.cast()` does not change the declared type of a variable. It only changes the static type of the expression it is applied to. You declared `a` as: ` `a: str | int = 1 ` ` so the type checker still regards the variable `a` as having the declared type `str | int` , even after assigning a casted expression back to it. Use a new variable, or cast at the point of use: ` `import typing a: str | int = 1 a_int: int = typing.cast(int, a) b: int = a_int # ok c: int = typing.cast(int, a) # also ok ` ` If you want real runtime narrowing, prefer `isinstance` / `assert` : ` `a: str | int = 1 assert isinstance(a, int) b: int = a ` `
label: explanatory_answer

text: You don't need to install `turtle` at all. The `turtle` graphics library built on top of `python-tk` is part of the Python 3 standard library. It doesn't require installation; it's already included with Python 3. The thing `pip` is trying to install is a much older library with the same name but built for a completely different purpose, which was never updated to be compatible with Python 3 in the first place. Just `import turtle` . You don't need to install anything first. (That said, note that you need `python3-tk` , not `python-tk` , to use turtle with Python 3). To demonstrate the above, see the file list for `libpython3.10-stdlib` for the Jammy release of Ubuntu (amd64); you'll see `/usr/lib/python3.10/turtle.py` included.

text: When you do ` `print(conclusion) ` ` Z3 is printing the internal representation for the expression. It's a Python based data-type representation of the expression. You're just seeing the expression as the Python layer represents it. When you do ` `print(conclusion.sexpr()) ` ` z3 prints the expression in the SMT-Lib format. SMT-Lib is the standard representation that's accepted by all compliant SMT solvers, as opposed to the AST you were seeing which is entirely z3 specific. So, these print differently, because in the first case you're getting the internal z3 specific AST that you can use for manipulating z3 expressions in Python, and in the latter case you're getting the standard representation understood by all solvers; which is an entirely different language.
label: explanatory_answer

text: Based on the Python documentation for the turtle module and the Errors and exceptions documentation, the answer is: do not `pip install turtle` `turtle` is part of Python’s standard library and the PyPI package named `turtle` is an unrelated, Python 2-era project that contains old exception syntax (e.g., `except ValueError, ve:` ) which causes `SyntaxError` under Python 3. Use the stdlib `turtle` directly; for example save and run: ` `# File 'example.py' import turtle t = turtle.Turtle() t.forward(100) turtle.done() # Then run: python example.py ` `

text: You can avoid the `KeyError` s in your example if you check whether the key already exists, and if it doesn't, set the key to have a value of one (since this is the first time that key has come up). You can do this a variety of ways. `'example' in dict` will resolve to a boolean showing if the key `example` is in the dictionary `dict` . Integrating this into your example code gives the following: ` `orders = ["Burger", "Pizza", "Burger", "Ramen", "Pizza", "Burger"] count_dict = {} for item in orders: if item in count_dict: # check for the key count_dict[item] += 1 # increment it if it exists else: # if it doesn't exist count_dict[item] = 1 # initialize the new key print(count_dict) ` ` You can do something similar with `dict.get(key)` , which returns the value if the key exists and `None` if it doesn't. Alternatively, you could use `try/except` to catch the `KeyError` s. Basic example: ` `try: dict[key] += 1 except KeyError: dict[key] = 1 ` ` While this works, I recommend using `in` or `get` as shown above. While a `KeyError` is fairly specific, it's easy to accidentally catch and handle an error that should've halted the program instead if you go about handling exceptions indis...

text: This code can be streamlined. You don't need two loggers. What you want is to log to different destinations based on the severity level. `handler.setLevel` can do that. However, while you can define the minimum logging level, you cannot directly set a maximum. So, if you do not want error messages to also show up in the general log, you should use filters. Here is how you could implement that (some code has been borrowed from Internet search): ` `import logging import logging.handlers class MaxLevelFilter(logging.Filter): """Filters out log messages with a severity higher than the maximum level.""" def __init__(self, max_level): super().__init__() self.max_level = max_level def filter(self, record): print(f"{record}, levelno: {record.levelno}, max_level: {self.max_level}") return record.levelno <= self.max_level logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG) formatter = logging.Formatter("%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(message)s") # this will include: INFO, WARNING, but not DEBUG handler = logging.FileHandler("info.log", mode="a", encoding="utf-8") handler.setLevel(logging.INFO) # minimum level handler.addFilter(MaxLevelFilter(logging.WARNING)) # maximum level...

Use parameters for the parts that vary. ` `def create_logger(level: str) -> logging.Logger: level_int = getattr(logging, level.upper()) l = logging.getLogger(f'{__name__}_{level}_') l.setLevel(level_int) handler = RotatingFileHandler(filename=f"{__name__}_{level}.log", maxBytes=5 * 1024 * 1024, backupCount=5) handler.namer = lambda name: name.replace(".log.", "") + ".log" handler.setFormatter(logging.Formatter("%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(message)s")) l.addHandler(handler) return l logger1 = create_logger('info') logger2 = create_logger('error') ` `
